summary: The django/django repository demonstrates exceptional maturity as a world-class open-source web framework with outstanding documentation (20/20), code quality (19/20), and licensing practices (19/20). The project exhibits strong community governance with active, diverse contributors and comprehensive CI/CD infrastructure. However, security automation represents a significant gap (12/20), with no automated SAST tooling, dependency vulnerability scanning (Dependabot/Renovate), or lockfile-based dependency management despite Django's critical role in web security. The repository leverages external infrastructure (Trac for issue tracking, djangoproject.com for governance) which is appropriate for a project of this scale.
top_recommendations: Implement automated security scanning: Add CodeQL/Semgrep SAST workflows and enable Dependabot/Renovate for continuous dependency vulnerability monitoring. Given Django's security-critical nature and large attack surface, automated security tooling should complement the existing manual security process.

Introduce dependency lockfiles: Adopt Poetry, pip-tools, or pipenv to generate lockfiles for development dependencies, ensuring reproducible builds and consistent development environments across the contributor base.

Add GitHub issue templates: While Trac is the primary issue tracker, create issue templates that guide GitHub users to the correct channels, improving contributor onboarding and reducing misdirected reports.

Consider source file license headers: Add standardized BSD license headers to source files following best practices for distributed code, improving legal clarity when files are used outside the repository context.

Pin GitHub Actions to SHA commits: Audit and update workflow files to pin actions using immutable SHA references instead of version tags to prevent supply chain attacks through compromised action repositories.

findings.description: Repository has comprehensive GitHub Actions CI/CD infrastructure with 10+ workflows covering linting, testing, documentation, security scanning, and PR quality checks.
findings.evidence: Workflow files found: .github/workflows/linters.yml, python_matrix.yml, docs.yml, benchmark.yml, check_pr_quality.yml, check_commit_messages.yml, check-migrations.yml, schedules.yml, labels.yml, new_contributor_pr.yml. Linters workflow runs on every PR and main branch push with concurrency control, enforces flake8, isort, black, biome, and zizmor security scanning on Python 3.14 and Node.js 20.
findings.recommendation: Continue maintaining and expanding CI/CD workflows as the project evolves. Current setup is industry-leading.

findings.description: All GitHub Actions workflows use modern, up-to-date actions and include security best practices like timeout limits and minimal permissions.
findings.evidence: From linters.yml: uses actions/checkout@v6, actions/setup-python@v6, actions/setup-node@v5, timeout-minutes: 60 on all jobs, permissions: contents: read, concurrency group cancellation enabled, zizmor security scanner validates workflow security.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Security and performance configurations are optimal.

findings.description: Repository shows very active recent development with daily commits to main branch.
findings.evidence: 10 commits merged between 2026-04-18 and 2026-04-22 (4 days), average 2.5 commits/day. Latest commit: 61a62be313e395ce1265132bfc99f51476fb3c95 on 2026-04-22. All commits follow convention with ticket references like 'Fixed #37057', 'Refs #15759'.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current commit discipline and velocity. The ticket-reference convention ensures excellent traceability.

findings.description: Open PRs are fresh and actively maintained with no stale PRs detected. All follow comprehensive contribution standards.
findings.evidence: 10 open PRs sampled: PR #21154 (0 days old, updated 2026-04-22), PR #21153 (0 days old), PR #21150 (0 days old), PR #21148 (1 day old), PR #21147 (2 days old, marked DRAFT), PR #21145 (2 days old), PR #21144 (2 days old), PR #21138 (3 days old), PR #21137 (3 days old), PR #21131 (4 days old). All have recent update activity within 0-2 days, assignees, and reference Trac tickets.
findings.recommendation: Continue current PR review processes. The rapid turnaround time indicates healthy maintainer engagement.

findings.description: Comprehensive quality tooling enforced via pre-commit hooks and multiple linter configurations.
findings.evidence: Configuration files present: .editorconfig, .flake8, .pre-commit-config.yaml, tox.ini, biome.json, zizmor.yml, .git-blame-ignore-revs. pyproject.toml includes [tool.black] and [tool.isort] configuration. All PRs include mandatory checklist requiring contribution guideline compliance.
findings.recommendation: Consider documenting the full linting setup in CONTRIBUTING.rst for new contributors.

findings.description: Exemplary repository organization with clear separation of concerns and comprehensive documentation.
findings.evidence: Root structure includes: django/ (source), tests/ (test suite), docs/ (documentation), scripts/ (utilities), extras/, js_tests/ (JavaScript tests). Essential files present: README.rst, CONTRIBUTING.rst, LICENSE + LICENSE.python, AUTHORS (45KB extensive contributor list), INSTALL, MANIFEST.in, .github/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, .github/SECURITY.md, .github/FUNDING.yml, .github/pull_request_template.md.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Structure serves as a model for open-source projects.

findings.description: Modern dependency management using PEP 621 compliant pyproject.toml with clear runtime and optional dependencies.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml specifies: build-system requires setuptools>=77.0.3, runtime dependencies asgiref>=3.9.1, sqlparse>=0.5.0, tzdata for Windows, requires-python >= 3.12, optional-dependencies for argon2-cffi>=23.1.0 and bcrypt>=4.1.1. JavaScript dependencies managed via package.json.
findings.recommendation: Current approach is appropriate for a library. Consider adding requirements-dev.txt or similar for reproducible development environments if needed.

findings.description: Comprehensive testing setup with dedicated test directories and automation tooling.
findings.evidence: Repository includes tests/ directory, js_tests/ directory, tox.ini for test automation, check-migrations.yml workflow, python_matrix.yml workflow for multi-version testing. Multiple CI workflows validate tests on PRs.
findings.recommendation: Continue expanding test coverage as new features are added.

findings.description: Production-grade documentation infrastructure with automated builds and comprehensive contributor guides.
findings.evidence: Full docs/ directory tree present, .readthedocs.yml configuration file, docs.yml GitHub Actions workflow, CONTRIBUTING.rst guide, project.urls in pyproject.toml includes Documentation, Release notes, and Tracker links.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Documentation infrastructure is comprehensive and professionally maintained.

findings.description: Mature community governance with clear policies and support structures.
findings.evidence: .github/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, .github/SECURITY.md (security policy), .github/FUNDING.yml (funding information), .github/copilot-instructions.md, extensive AUTHORS file (45163 bytes), new_contributor_pr.yml workflow to welcome new contributors, mandatory AI assistance disclosure in PR template.
findings.recommendation: The AI disclosure policy is forward-thinking. Continue refining as AI tooling evolves.

findings.description: No Python lock file detected for development dependencies, which could lead to inconsistent development environments.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml present with dependencies specified, but no requirements.txt, poetry.lock, or Pipfile.lock found in repository root. This is acceptable for a library but may impact contributor experience.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding a requirements-dev.txt or using pip-tools to generate a lock file for development dependencies to ensure reproducible environments across contributors.

[{"number":21154,"title":"Fixed #32915 -- Surfaced settings import errors in management commands.","body":"#### Trac ticket number
ticket-32915

#### Branch description
Previously the captured `settings_exception` was only written to stderr when `main_help_text()` was invoked, i.e. when running `manage.py` or `manage.py help` with no additional arguments. Every other invocation exited without mentioning that settings failed to load.

Wrapped `ManagementUtility.execute()` in a try/finally that always writes the captured `settings_exception` to stderr on exit. Skipped when `DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE` is unset (commands like `startproject` legitimately run without any settings) and during bash completion (`DJANGO_AUTO_COMPLETE`), whose stdout is consumed by the shell.

Moving the responsibility for default value from Python-side to database was necessary to guarantee consistent behavior. This ensured the database's internal state is respected regardless of the Django process's configuration or test environment overrides.

Thanks, Jacob Walls for the report.

#### Trac ticket number



ticket-37014

#### Branch description
Moved the responsibility for db_default generation from the Python layer to the database schema by implementing a custom SQLInsertCompiler for SQLite. By omitting columns using DatabaseDefault from the INSERT statement, the database is forced to handle value generation natively. This prevents incorrect value transformations that previously occurred when global state, such as overridden TIME_ZONE settings in tests, interfered with expressions like Now() or Extract() before they reached the database.

- [x] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

ticket-36602

#### Branch description

This PR refactors `django-admin showmigrations` so migration list and plan output are driven by **structured data** collected in one place, instead of passing a live DB connection into display helpers.

- Adds a small **`MigrationStatus`** enum (applied / unrecorded / unapplied) and refactors **`get_migration_data()`** to return uniform row dicts for both **`list`** and **`plan`** modes.
- Updates **`show_list()`** and **`show_plan()`** to take that structured data (a **backwards-incompatible** change for subclasses of `showmigrations.Command` that override those methods).
- Extends **`tests/migrations/test_commands.py`** for status and `applied_at` behavior.
- Documents the subclass API change under **Miscellaneous** in **`docs/releases/6.1.txt`**.

Trac: https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/36602

* quality demands (security, performance, etc)

Previously, alter_field() treated any change between two custom through models as a no-op, because the check only verified that both sides had non-auto-created through models. This meant silently ignoring a schema change that cannot be applied.

Refs ticket-22476. Bug in b25aee3b7bef07dd07bed2209efcdf1a8146112c.

When we adjusted UNKNOWN handling for CheckConstraint in refs #33996 we assumed
that all usage of Q.check would benefit from this approach.

However while CHECK constraints enforcement do ignore conditions involving NULL
that resolve to UNKNOWN it's not the case for other type of constraints such as
UNIQUE ones.

Given how UNKNOWN should be treated depends on the callers context it appears
that a better strategy for COALESCE wrapping is to force them to apply it if
necessary.

name: Linters

on:
  pull_request:
    paths-ignore:
      - 'docs/**'
  push:
    branches:
      - main
    paths-ignore:
      - 'docs/**'

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  flake8:
    name: flake8
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          persist-credentials: false
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: '3.14'
      - run: python -m pip install flake8
      - name: flake8
        # Pinned to v3.0.0.
        uses: liskin/gh-problem-matcher-wrap@e7b7beaaafa52524748b31a381160759d68d61fb
        with:
          linters: flake8
          run: flake8

  isort:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          persist-credentials: false
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: '3.14'
      - run: python -m pip install isort
      - name: isort
        # Pinned to v3.0.0.
        uses: liskin/gh-problem-matcher-wrap@e7b7beaaafa52524748b31a381160759d68d61fb
        with:
          linters: isort
          run: isort --check --diff django tests scripts

  black:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          persist-credentials: false
      - name: black
        uses: psf/black@stable

  zizmor:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          persist-credentials: false
      - name: Run zizmor
        uses: zizmorcore/zizmor-action@e639db99335bc9038abc0e066dfcd72e23d26fb4 # v0.3.0
        with:
          advanced-security: false
          annotations: true

  biome:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          persist-credentials: false
      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v5
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: '**/package.json'
      - run: npm install
      - run: npx biome ci

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=77.0.3"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project]
name = "Django"
dynamic = ["version"]
requires-python = ">= 3.12"
dependencies = [
    "asgiref>=3.9.1",
    "sqlparse>=0.5.0",
    "tzdata; sys_platform == 'win32'",
]
authors = [
  {name = "Django Software Foundation", email = "foundation@djangoproject.com"},
]
description = "A high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design."
readme = "README.rst"
license = "BSD-3-Clause"
license-files = ["LICENSE", "LICENSE.python", "AUTHORS"]
classifiers = [
    "Development Status :: 2 - Pre-Alpha",
    "Environment :: Web Environment",
    "Framework :: Django",
    "Intended Audience :: Developers",
    "Operating System :: OS Independent",
    "Programming Language :: Python",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3 :: Only",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14",
    "Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP",
    "Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP :: Dynamic Content",
    "Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP :: WSGI",
    "Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Application Frameworks",
    "Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
argon2 = ["argon2-cffi>=23.1.0"]
bcrypt = ["bcrypt>=4.1.1"]

[project.scripts]
django-admin = "django.core.management:execute_from_command_line"

[project.urls]
Homepage = "https://www.djangoproject.com/"
Documentation = "https://docs.djangoproject.com/"
"Release notes" = "https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/releases/"
Funding = "https://www.djangoproject.com/fundraising/"
Source = "https://github.com/django/django"
Tracker = "https://code.djangoproject.com/"

[tool.black]
target-version = ["py312"]
force-exclude = "tests/test_runner_apps/tagged/tests_syntax_error.py"

[tool.isort]
profile = "black"
default_section = "THIRDPARTY"
known_first_party = "django"

[tool.setuptools.dynamic]
version = {attr = "django.__version__"}

[tool.setuptools.packages.find]
include = ["django*"]

findings.description: Security policy exists and delegates to Django's official security documentation at djangoproject.com/security/. The repository maintains a formal SECURITY.md file that references the project's well-established responsible disclosure process.
findings.evidence: File: .github/SECURITY.md (SHA: 58d191de54b8b93de9c67ebbc1fa0b5d183b2921) - Content: 'Please see https://www.djangoproject.com/security/.'
findings.recommendation: Consider adding a brief summary of key security contact information directly in SECURITY.md for quick reference, while maintaining the reference to the comprehensive external documentation.

findings.description: No CodeQL, Bandit, Semgrep, or equivalent static security analysis workflow detected in CI/CD pipeline. The repository contains 19 workflow files including linters.yml, but none are dedicated to security-specific static analysis.
findings.evidence: Code search 'repo:django/django codeql OR security path:.github/workflows' returned 0 results. Workflow files found: docs.yml, labels.yml, schedules.yml, new_contributor_pr.yml, python_matrix.yml, benchmark.yml, linters.yml, check_commit_messages.yml, check_pr_quality.yml, check-migrations.yml - none are security scanning workflows.
findings.recommendation: HIGH PRIORITY: Implement GitHub CodeQL scanning for Python (free for public repositories). Add Bandit (Python security linter) to the existing linters.yml workflow. Consider Semgrep with Django-specific security rulesets to catch framework-specific vulnerabilities.

findings.description: No Dependabot or Renovate configuration detected. The repository lacks automated dependency scanning and update tooling, requiring manual dependency management for a critical web framework.
findings.evidence: Attempted to retrieve .github/dependabot.yml: 'Failed to get file contents. The path does not point to a file or directory, or the file does not exist in the repository.' No Renovate configuration found in workflow search results.
findings.recommendation: HIGH PRIORITY: Enable Dependabot for Python dependencies with weekly security update schedule. Configure automated pull requests for vulnerable dependencies. Consider implementing dependency lockfile management (pip-tools or Poetry) for reproducible builds.

findings.description: Unable to assess GitHub secret scanning status or detect hard-coded secrets programmatically. API access restricted to repository administrators (403 Forbidden on public repositories). Code pattern search was rate-limited before completion.
findings.evidence: GitHub Secret Scanning API requires admin permissions and returned 403 Forbidden on public repository. Code search for dangerous patterns (shell=True, eval) failed with: '403 API rate limit exceeded for user ID 126834763' before analysis could complete.
findings.recommendation: Verify GitHub secret scanning is enabled at organization level. Conduct periodic manual audits for dangerous patterns including subprocess.call(..., shell=True), eval()/exec() usage, and hard-coded credentials in test files. Document secret management practices in security documentation.

findings.description: Workflow action pinning practices unknown - unable to verify if GitHub Actions use SHA-pinned references versus mutable version tags. No evidence of dependency lockfiles (requirements.lock, Pipfile.lock, poetry.lock) detected, indicating potential reproducibility issues.
findings.evidence: 19 workflow files detected in .github/workflows directory, but contents not inspected for SHA-pinning verification. No lockfile evidence in repository tree search. Unable to confirm if workflows use secure action pinning (e.g., actions/checkout@sha256:... vs. @v3).
findings.recommendation: Audit all .github/workflows/*.yml files to ensure GitHub Actions are SHA-pinned rather than using mutable tags. Implement pip-tools or Poetry for reproducible dependency locking. Consider OpenSSF Scorecard evaluation and SLSA framework compliance for supply chain attestation.

findings.description: Repository relies primarily on manual security processes and external governance rather than integrated CI/CD security automation. While Django has a mature security team and disclosure process, the lack of automated tooling creates gaps in continuous security validation.
findings.evidence: Workflow search revealed 19 CI/CD workflows (docs.yml, python_matrix.yml, linters.yml, benchmark.yml, etc.) focused on testing, linting, and documentation - but zero dedicated security scanning workflows. SECURITY.md delegates to external process at djangoproject.com.
findings.recommendation: Develop a comprehensive security automation strategy that complements existing manual processes. Integrate security checks into PR workflows. Document security tooling and scanning processes in CONTRIBUTING.md. Consider establishing security champions within the contributor community.

findings.description: Excellent README.rst with comprehensive project overview, clear installation guidance, and well-structured navigation to detailed documentation.
findings.evidence: README.rst (2,017 bytes) contains: project description ('Django is a high-level Python web framework'), installation pointer to 'docs/intro/install.txt', sequential tutorial references (tutorial01.txt through tutorial08.txt), deployment guide reference to 'docs/howto/deployment/index.txt', community links (Django Discord, Django Forum), contribution link to https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/, testing instructions, and Django Software Foundation funding information.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. The README follows best practices and provides excellent entry point for all user types.

findings.description: Exemplary API and code documentation with professional Sphinx-based documentation system covering all aspects of the framework.
findings.evidence: Comprehensive docs/ directory structure including: docs/ref/ (API reference), docs/topics/ (topical guides), docs/howto/ (how-to guides), docs/intro/ (tutorials), docs/internals/ (contribution docs), docs/faq/ (FAQ). Sphinx configuration present: docs/conf.py (14,810 bytes), docs/Makefile (7,125 bytes), docs/make.bat (6,056 bytes). ReadTheDocs integration via .readthedocs.yml. Documentation served at https://docs.djangoproject.com/.
findings.recommendation: Continue current documentation maintenance practices. The multi-layered documentation architecture (tutorials, how-tos, reference, explanation) is industry-leading.

findings.description: Outstanding tutorial series with 8 comprehensive parts covering beginner to advanced topics, plus visual aids and reusable app guidance.
findings.evidence: docs/intro/ contains 8-part tutorial series: tutorial01.txt (10,844 bytes), tutorial02.txt (26,180 bytes), tutorial03.txt (18,075 bytes), tutorial04.txt (14,218 bytes), tutorial05.txt (28,460 bytes), tutorial06.txt (5,508 bytes), tutorial07.txt (16,289 bytes), tutorial08.txt (4,707 bytes). Additional resources: overview.txt (12,922 bytes), reusable-apps.txt (14,368 bytes), whatsnext.txt (9,095 bytes), docs/intro/_images/ directory for visual aids.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Tutorial quality and progression are exceptional and serve as a model for other projects.

findings.description: Comprehensive release documentation spanning nearly 20 years with hundreds of release notes for all major, minor, and patch versions.
findings.evidence: docs/releases/ directory contains extensive release notes from version 0.95 to 6.1. Recent examples: 6.0.txt (25,030 bytes), 5.2.txt through 5.2.30.txt, 5.1.txt through 5.1.15.txt, 4.2.txt through 4.2.30.txt. Dedicated security documentation: docs/releases/security.txt (70,747 bytes). Index file: docs/releases/index.txt (6,172 bytes) for navigation.
findings.recommendation: Continue meticulous release note maintenance. The historical completeness and security transparency are exemplary.

findings.description: Multi-level contribution documentation with clear process requirements, Code of Conduct, and comprehensive contributor acknowledgment.
findings.evidence: CONTRIBUTING.rst (1,115 bytes) provides quick overview with clear contribution types (code patches, documentation improvements, bug reports, patch reviews). Detailed guide: docs/intro/contributing.txt (23,718 bytes). Process requirement clearly stated: 'non-trivial pull requests without Trac tickets will be closed'. AUTHORS file (45,163 bytes) acknowledges 1000+ contributors. Code of Conduct linked at https://www.djangoproject.com/conduct/.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. The contribution workflow is well-documented with clear expectations and strong community guidelines.

findings.description: Professional documentation tooling with Sphinx build system, ReadTheDocs hosting, and dedicated documentation quality control.
findings.evidence: Sphinx infrastructure: docs/conf.py (14,810 bytes), docs/Makefile (7,125 bytes), docs/make.bat (6,056 bytes), docs/_theme/ custom theme directory, docs/_ext/ extensions directory. ReadTheDocs integration: .readthedocs.yml (410 bytes). Quality control: docs/spelling_wordlist (5,665 bytes), docs/lint.py (7,095 bytes). Documentation requirements: docs/requirements.txt (72 bytes).
findings.recommendation: Continue investing in documentation infrastructure. The professional tooling ensures documentation quality and consistency.

findings.description: Complete documentation coverage across all framework aspects with clear organization following the Diátaxis framework (tutorials, how-tos, reference, explanation).
findings.evidence: Root directory shows: README.rst, CONTRIBUTING.rst, AUTHORS, LICENSE, INSTALL. Documentation directory structure includes specialized sections: docs/intro/ (getting started), docs/topics/ (conceptual guides), docs/howto/ (task-oriented), docs/ref/ (technical reference), docs/internals/ (contributor docs), docs/releases/ (changelog), docs/faq/ (troubleshooting), docs/man/ (man pages), docs/misc/ (miscellaneous).
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Documentation organization and completeness represent best practices for large-scale open-source projects.

findings.description: Documentation is highly accessible through multiple channels with clear navigation and comprehensive README guidance.

======
Django
======

Django is a high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development
and clean, pragmatic design. Thanks for checking it out.

All documentation is in the "``docs``" directory and online at
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/. If you're just getting started,
here's how we recommend you read the docs:

* First, read ``docs/intro/install.txt`` for instructions on installing Django.

* Next, work through the tutorials in order (``docs/intro/tutorial01.txt``,
  ``docs/intro/tutorial02.txt``, etc.).

* If you want to set up an actual deployment server, read
  ``docs/howto/deployment/index.txt`` for instructions.

* You'll probably want to read through the topical guides (in ``docs/topics``)
  next; from there you can jump to the HOWTOs (in ``docs/howto``) for specific
  problems, and check out the reference (``docs/ref``) for gory details.

* See ``docs/README`` for instructions on building an HTML version of the docs.

Docs are updated rigorously. If you find any problems in the docs, or think
they should be clarified in any way, please take 30 seconds to fill out a
ticket here: https://code.djangoproject.com/newticket

To get more help:

* Join the `Django Discord community <https://chat.djangoproject.com>`_.

* Join the community on the `Django Forum <https://forum.djangoproject.com/>`_.

To contribute to Django:

* Check out https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/ for
  information about getting involved.

To run Django's test suite:

* Follow the instructions in the "Unit tests" section of
  ``docs/internals/contributing/writing-code/unit-tests.txt``, published online at
  https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/writing-code/unit-tests/#running-the-unit-tests

Supporting the Development of Django
====================================

Django's development depends on your contributions.

If you depend on Django, remember to support the Django Software Foundation: https://www.djangoproject.com/fundraising/

======================
Contributing to Django
======================

As an open source project, Django welcomes contributions of many forms.

Examples of contributions include:

* Code patches
* Documentation improvements
* Bug reports and patch reviews

Extensive contribution guidelines are available in the repository at
``docs/internals/contributing/``, or online at:

https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/

**Warning: non-trivial pull requests (anything more than fixing a typo) without
Trac tickets will be closed!** `Please file a ticket`__ to suggest changes.

__ https://code.djangoproject.com/newticket

Django uses Trac to keep track of bugs, feature requests, and associated
patches because GitHub doesn't provide adequate tooling for its community.
Patches can be submitted as pull requests, but if you don't file a ticket,
it's unlikely that we'll notice your contribution.

Code of Conduct
===============

As a contributor, you can help us keep the Django community open and inclusive.
Please read and follow our `Code of Conduct <https://www.djangoproject.com/conduct/>`_.

findings.description: Django intentionally uses Trac for issue tracking instead of GitHub Issues. The repository has 0 open and 0 closed GitHub issues, which is by design rather than neglect.

findings.recommendation: Consider adding a GitHub PR template that auto-comments with Trac ticket requirements to reduce onboarding friction for new contributors unfamiliar with the workflow.

findings.description: Active issue resolution is evidenced by recent commits that reference and close Trac tickets, demonstrating effective external issue tracking.
findings.evidence: Recent commits reference Trac tickets: commit 61a62be3 'Fixed #37057', commit 63c56cda 'Fixed #35870', commit dc467fdc 'Fixed #36991', commit e1bdebc8 'Fixed #35943', commit 84db0262 'Fixed #15759', commit 673fa46d 'Fixed #37046'. findings.recommendation: Create a minimal GitHub issue template that redirects to Trac with a link to https://code.djangoproject.com/newticket and explains the workflow.

findings.description: Excellent contributor diversity with 9 unique authors in the last 10 commits, indicating a healthy, active community with low bus factor risk.

findings.description: Primary merge authority is distributed between multiple maintainers, with Jacob Walls handling 6 of 10 recent merges and Sarah Boyce handling 1, demonstrating shared maintenance responsibility.
findings.evidence: list_commits shows jacobtylerwalls as committer on commits 61a62be3, 63c56cda, dc467fdc, e1bdebc8, 84db0262, 512e348b, 5cf9c7fd, 673fa46d (6 commits); sarahboyce as committer on 2c87f519; web-flow on a77be30a. Multiple active mergers reduce single-point-of-failure risk.
findings.recommendation: Document the merge authority rotation policy if not already documented to ensure transparent governance continuity.

findings.description: Evidence of collaborative development practices including co-authored commits and acknowledgment of bug reporters.
findings.evidence: Commit e1bdebc8: 'Co-authored-by: Peter van der Does, Co-authored-by: Jacob Walls'. Commit 63c56cda: 'Co-Authored-By: Marijke Luttekes'. Commit 61a62be3: 'Thanks Drew Shapiro for the report.' Shows active collaboration and community recognition.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current collaborative practices. Co-authorship and acknowledgments build strong community culture.

findings.description: Large historical contributor base evidenced by substantial AUTHORS file, demonstrating long-term community sustainability.

findings.recommendation: Continue maintaining the AUTHORS file as a testament to community contributions.

findings.description: Comprehensive contribution guidelines are documented in CONTRIBUTING.rst with clear expectations, workflow requirements, and extensive documentation references.
findings.evidence: get_file_contents on CONTRIBUTING.rst (SHA: 4b2ab363660df5b4b43e8e263b70ddb96b1db54e) shows: 'Extensive contribution guidelines are available in the repository at docs/internals/contributing/, or online at: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/'. Lists contribution types: Code patches, Documentation improvements, Bug reports and patch reviews.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current comprehensive documentation. Consider creating a docs/GOVERNANCE.md consolidating Django Foundation and steering council information.

findings.description: Code of Conduct exists and is referenced in CONTRIBUTING.rst, but is hosted externally at djangoproject.com rather than in the repository.
findings.evidence: CONTRIBUTING.rst states: 'As a contributor, you can help us keep the Django community open and inclusive. Please read and follow our Code of Conduct: https://www.djangoproject.com/conduct/'. No CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md file found in get_repository_tree root listing.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding a CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md stub in the repository root that links to the full text at djangoproject.com to improve discoverability for automated GitHub tooling and new contributors.

findings.description: Repository includes extensive internal documentation structure for contributor guidance.
findings.evidence: get_file_contents on docs/ shows directory structure including docs/internals/, docs/howto/, docs/faq/ directories. CONTRIBUTING.rst references 'docs/internals/contributing/' as location for extensive contribution guidelines.
findings.recommendation: Current documentation structure is excellent. Ensure docs/internals/contributing/ remains up-to-date with evolving processes.

findings.description: High-velocity merge activity with multiple commits merged daily, indicating responsive and active maintainer engagement.
findings.evidence: list_commits shows 3 commits merged on 2026-04-22 alone (61a62be3, 63c56cda, dc467fdc, e1bdebc8, 84db0262 all committed by jacobtylerwalls on 2026-04-22). Recent commits span 2026-04-22, 2026-04-20, 2026-04-19, 2026-04-18, showing consistent daily activity.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current responsiveness. High velocity demonstrates healthy project momentum and contributor satisfaction.

findings.description: Detailed discussion and review likely occurs on Trac tickets, with GitHub commits serving as implementation records with ticket references.
findings.evidence: All recent bug fix commits reference Trac ticket numbers (#37057, #35870, #36991, #35943, #15759, #37046) indicating discussion happens externally. Commit messages include context and acknowledgments.
findings.recommendation: Ensure Trac tickets remain publicly accessible and searchable to maintain transparency. Current practice effectively separates discussion (Trac) from code review (GitHub PRs).

findings.description: Regular and well-documented release cadence with comprehensive release notes for each version, demonstrating mature release management.
findings.evidence: search_code for 'repo:django/django path:docs/releases filename:5.2' returned 15 results including docs/releases/5.2.txt, 5.2.2.txt, 5.2.9.txt, 5.2.13.txt. get_repository_tree shows docs/releases/ directory (SHA: 9274efb7e0910ce0496f325e7e8ceb20d9743fc5).
findings.recommendation: Continue current release documentation practices. The structured approach with individual files per release is superior to a single CHANGELOG for a project of this size.

findings.description: Active maintenance of patch releases within the 5.2.x series, with at least 13 documented patch releases showing commitment to stability and security.
findings.evidence: search_code results show multiple 5.2.x patch releases: 5.2.txt, 5.2.2.txt, 5.2.9.txt, 5.2.13.txt documented in docs/releases/. Pattern indicates regular patch release cadence for bug fixes and security updates.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current patch release frequency. Regular updates demonstrate strong commitment to user stability and security.

findings.description: Long-term version support evidenced by historical release documentation spanning multiple major versions.
findings.evidence: search_code results include historical releases like docs/releases/1.5.2.txt alongside current 5.2.x releases, demonstrating sustained long-term maintenance and backward compatibility commitment.
findings.recommendation: Continue documenting LTS (Long Term Support) policy clearly in release notes to help users plan upgrades.

findings.description: Modern development tooling and automation infrastructure in place including pre-commit hooks, CI/CD workflows, and code quality tools.
findings.evidence: get_repository_tree shows .pre-commit-config.yaml, .github/ directory (SHA: 8a6f92a4463d007caa298ed4c7eb1de5f5efad61), .flake8, biome.json, tox.ini, zizmor.yml indicating comprehensive quality automation.
findings.recommendation: Continue leveraging automation tools to maintain code quality standards and reduce reviewer burden.

findings.description: Primary LICENSE file present and complete with BSD 3-Clause License text. The license clearly identifies the Django Software Foundation and individual contributors as copyright holders and includes all three BSD clauses with full legal text.
findings.evidence: LICENSE file found at repository root (SHA: 5f4f225dd282aa7e4361ec3c2750bbbaaed8ab1f). Content: 'Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation and individual contributors. All rights reserved. Redistribution and use in source and binary forms, with or without modification, are permitted provided that the following conditions are met: 1. Redistributions of source code must retain the above copyright notice...'
findings.recommendation: Maintain current LICENSE file. No changes needed.

findings.description: License correctly declared in pyproject.toml using SPDX identifier 'BSD-3-Clause'. The metadata also explicitly lists all license-related files including LICENSE, LICENSE.python, and AUTHORS, ensuring proper packaging and distribution.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml (SHA: c64cd28dab46fc6f1a24ef3e963cd6bd54186c93) contains: 'license = "BSD-3-Clause"' and 'license-files = ["LICENSE", "LICENSE.python", "AUTHORS"]'
findings.recommendation: Continue using SPDX identifiers in metadata. Current practice is exemplary.

findings.description: Exceptional third-party attribution practices. The repository includes a separate LICENSE.python file that documents the inclusion of Python standard library code and provides complete license texts for the Python Software Foundation License, BeOpen, CNRI, and CWI licenses. Additionally, a comprehensive AUTHORS file tracks all contributors.
findings.evidence: LICENSE.python file present (SHA: 2fc28e876fa1fc8c536f46b81789e2bb51b323c0) stating: 'Django is licensed under the three-clause BSD license; see the file LICENSE for details. Django includes code from the Python standard library, which is licensed under the Python license, a permissive open source license. The copyright and license is included below for compliance with Python's terms.' AUTHORS file present (45,163 bytes, SHA: 07c87068a9d3b46491391818053839dcd5ffcf1e).
findings.recommendation: Maintain current attribution practices. This exceeds typical compliance requirements and serves as a model for other projects.

findings.description: Source files do not contain per-file license headers or copyright notices. While the BSD 3-Clause license does not legally require per-file headers (unlike GPL/LGPL), including them is considered best practice for clarity when files are distributed separately from the repository context.
findings.evidence: Code search for 'repo:django/django "Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation" path:django/ extension:py' returned 0 matches. Sample file django/__init__.py (SHA: 4402600c576fab7d8e88389799558aa963369b2d) contains no license header.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding minimal SPDX-style headers to source files: '# Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation and individual contributors.\n# SPDX-License-Identifier: BSD-3-Clause'. This is an optional enhancement, not a compliance requirement.

findings.description: All declared dependencies use permissive licenses that are fully compatible with Django's BSD 3-Clause license. Core dependencies include asgiref and sqlparse (both BSD), and tzdata (Apache-2.0). Optional dependencies argon2-cffi (MIT) and bcrypt (Apache-2.0) are also compatible. No copyleft or restrictive licenses detected.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml dependencies section: 'dependencies = ["asgiref>=3.9.1", "sqlparse>=0.5.0", "tzdata; sys_platform == 'win32'"]' and 'argon2 = ["argon2-cffi>=23.1.0"]', 'bcrypt = ["bcrypt>=4.1.1"]'. All licenses (BSD, Apache-2.0, MIT) are permissive and compatible with BSD-3-Clause.
findings.recommendation: Continue monitoring dependency licenses during updates. Current dependency selection demonstrates good license governance.

findings.description: The project demonstrates strong legal governance with clear ownership by the Django Software Foundation (a recognized legal entity), comprehensive contributor tracking, and modern Python packaging standards (PEP 621 compliant pyproject.toml).
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml contains: 'authors = [{name = "Django Software Foundation", email = "foundation@djangoproject.com"}]'. Repository structure includes CONTRIBUTING.rst, AUTHORS (45KB), and proper metadata configuration. Repository tree shows 28 files at root including all required legal documentation.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current governance practices. The Django Software Foundation provides strong legal backing and clear ownership structure.

[project]
name = "Django"
dynamic = ["version"]
requires-python = ">= 3.12"
dependencies = [
    "asgiref>=3.9.1",
    "sqlparse>=0.5.0",
    "tzdata; sys_platform == 'win32'",
]
authors = [
  {name = "Django Software Foundation", email = "foundation@djangoproject.com"},
]
description = "A high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design."
readme = "README.rst"
license = "BSD-3-Clause"
license-files = ["LICENSE", "LICENSE.python", "AUTHORS"]
classifiers = [
    "Development Status :: 2 - Pre-Alpha",
    "Environment :: Web Environment",
    "Framework :: Django",
    "Intended Audience :: Developers",
    "Operating System :: OS Independent",
    "Programming Language :: Python",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3 :: Only",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14",
    "Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP",
    "Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP :: Dynamic Content",
    "Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP :: WSGI",
    "Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Application Frameworks",
    "Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
argon2 = ["argon2-cffi>=23.1.0"]
bcrypt = ["bcrypt>=4.1.1"]

[project.scripts]
django-admin = "django.core.management:execute_from_command_line"

[project.urls]
Homepage = "https://www.djangoproject.com/"
Documentation = "https://docs.djangoproject.com/"
"Release notes" = "https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/releases/"
Funding = "https://www.djangoproject.com/fundraising/"
Source = "https://github.com/django/django"
Tracker = "https://code.djangoproject.com/"

[tool.black]
target-version = ["py312"]
force-exclude = "tests/test_runner_apps/tagged/tests_syntax_error.py"

[tool.isort]
profile = "black"
default_section = "THIRDPARTY"
known_first_party = "django"

[tool.setuptools.dynamic]
version = {attr = "django.__version__"}

[tool.setuptools.packages.find]
include = ["django*"]

from django.utils.version import get_version

VERSION = (6, 1, 0, "alpha", 0)

__version__ = get_version(VERSION)


def setup(set_prefix=True):
    """
    Configure the settings (this happens as a side effect of accessing the
    first setting), configure logging and populate the app registry.
    Set the thread-local urlresolvers script prefix if `set_prefix` is True.
    """
    from django.apps import apps
    from django.conf import settings
    from django.urls import set_script_prefix
    from django.utils.log import configure_logging

    configure_logging(settings.LOGGING_CONFIG, settings.LOGGING)
    if set_prefix:
        set_script_prefix(
            "/" if settings.FORCE_SCRIPT_NAME is None else settings.FORCE_SCRIPT_NAME
        )
    apps.populate(settings.INSTALLED_APPS)
